title: User Feedback Loops
concept: Capturing thumbs up/down or corrections to build a growing evaluation and fine-tuning dataset over time.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, how did we detect silent quality regressions before they impacted users?

options.text: Waiting for users to email support about broken links.
Tracking shifts in retrieval score distributions and answer lengths.
Restarting the LLM server every hour to clear the cache.

exercises.explain: Monitoring silent quality regressions involves looking for systemic shifts in metrics (like a sudden drop in retrieval similarity scores or changes in average answer length) before users start complaining.

cards.title: Listen to Your Users
cards.body: No automated test can perfectly match how real humans use your app. By setting up feedback loops, your users will actively tell you what they love and where the model is failing!

cards.title: What is a User Feedback Loop?
cards.body: A User Feedback Loop is a system that captures explicit signals (like thumbs up/down) and implicit signals (like copy-pasting or editing an answer) from users. This data is stored alongside the prompt and response to build a dataset for evaluation and model fine-tuning.

cards.title: The Suggestion Box Analogy
cards.body: Think of user feedback loops like a suggestion box at a bakery. Instead of guessing if the new bread recipe is good, you let customers leave quick notes so you can adjust the recipe for the next batch.

cards.title: Feedback in Action
cards.body: A customer support chatbot adds a small 'thumbs down' button. If a user clicks it and types 'This answer was too technical', the system logs the prompt, the response, and the correction to help developers fine-tune the next model version.

cards.title: Simple beats Complex
cards.body: Many believe you need complex surveys to get user feedback. In reality, simple interactions like clicking a thumbs-up or editing a generated response provide highly accurate data with almost zero user effort.

cards.title: Feedback Evolution
cards.body: Comparing how raw user reactions are transformed into valuable training assets.

left.text: A simple click indicating the answer was unhelpful.

right.text: Prompt + bad response + user correction saved to database.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is an example of explicit user feedback?

options.text: Measuring how long the user stays on the page
Clicking a thumbs-down button on a generated answer
Analyzing the length of the model's response
Logging the time of day the user sent the query

exercises.explain: Explicit feedback requires the user to make a conscious choice to evaluate the output, such as clicking a rating button or typing a correction.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To build a useful fine-tuning dataset, you only need to save the user's rating (like thumbs up) without saving the original prompt.
exercises.explain: Collecting raw ratings is only the first step. To use feedback for fine-tuning or evaluation, you must log it along with the original user prompt and the model's generated response.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of feedback collection.
exercises.sentence: Every thumbs-up and correction you save helps build a golden dataset for training better models.
exercises.explain: By saving user ratings alongside the prompts and model outputs, you create a valuable dataset that can be used to evaluate and fine-tune future models.

exercises.prompt: Identify the feedback log entry that was likely generated by an AI rather than a real human user.

options.text: User feedback: 'The tone was too aggressive, please make it polite.'
System log: 'Feedback payload: {"rating": -1, "suggested_edit": "SystemError: Code execution failed on line 42 with NullPointerException due to invalid stack trace reference."}'
User feedback: 'Thumbs up, this solved my billing question!'

exercises.explain: Option B is written by an AI because it contains a hallucinated, overly complex response that includes fictional software code instead of a simple user correction.

exercises.prompt: You want to use positive and negative user feedback to permanently improve your LLM application's performance over time. What is the most effective way to utilize this collected feedback data?

options.text: It automatically retrains the model in real-time after every single thumbs-down.
It builds a curated dataset of inputs and outputs to fine-tune and evaluate future models.
It guarantees that users will never experience another hallucination.
It replaces the need for automated monitoring metrics like latency and token count.

exercises.explain: Capturing explicit ratings alongside the exact input prompt and output response allows you to curate a high-quality dataset of successes and failures. This dataset is the primary fuel needed to fine-tune your model to avoid those specific mistakes in the future.
